title: Shrimp and Grits
subtitle: Low Country shrimp in a spiced sauce over creamy stone-ground grits
description: Shrimp and grits is the defining dish of the South Carolina Low Country: large prawns cooked quickly with bacon, garlic, and a spiced tomato-based sauce, served over a bowl of creamy, butter-enriched stone-ground grits. The grits take the most time — they need at least twenty minutes of gentle cooking to become smooth and porridge-like. The shrimp sauce is assembled in ten minutes once the grits are on. Together they form one of the great American regional dishes.
sourceNotes: Shrimp and grits has roots in the Gullah Geechee culture of the South Carolina and Georgia Low Country. The dish was popularised nationally by Bill Neal at Crook's Corner restaurant in Chapel Hill, North Carolina, in the early 1980s, and described in Craig Claiborne's New York Times column in 1985.

recipeTools.notes: For the grits — a heavy-based pan prevents scorching during the long, slow cook.
For the shrimp sauce — wide enough to cook the prawns in a single layer.

glossaryTerms.definition: Ground dried hominy corn (maize), cooked with water or stock into a thick porridge. The Southern American equivalent of Italian polenta. Stone-ground grits retain more flavour and texture than quick-cook or instant varieties.

ingredients.notes: Not instant — stone-ground gives the best flavour and texture
Or a mix of water and whole milk
Sharp cheddar, grated — optional but traditional
Peeled and deveined — about 16–20 large prawns
Sliced, white and green parts separated

steps: Start the grits: bring the water to the boil in a heavy saucepan. Add salt. Whisk in the grits in a slow, steady stream to prevent lumps. Reduce to the lowest heat and cook for 25–30 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes, until thick and creamy. If they become too thick, add splashes of hot water. Finish with the butter and cheddar if using. Keep warm.
While the grits cook, render the bacon in a large frying pan over a medium heat for 5–6 minutes until crisp. Remove with a slotted spoon, leaving the fat behind.
Increase the heat to medium-high. Add the white parts of the spring onions and the green pepper to the bacon fat. Cook for 3 minutes. Add the garlic and smoked paprika for 1 minute.
Add the tinned tomatoes, chicken stock, Worcestershire sauce, and hot sauce. Simmer for 5 minutes until slightly thickened.
Season the prawns with salt and pepper. Add them to the sauce and cook for 2–3 minutes, turning once, until pink and just cooked through. Do not overcook.
Remove from the heat. Add the lemon juice and the crisp bacon. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Spoon the warm grits into bowls. Top with the shrimp and sauce. Scatter with the green parts of the spring onions and the parsley.

content.text: first — they take 25–30 minutes and everything else can be assembled while they cook. Stone-ground grits need constant low heat and occasional stirring; the reward is a deeply flavoured, textured porridge. The shrimp sauce assembles in under fifteen minutes and should be made while the grits finish.

Bring {{water}} of water to the boil. Add salt. Whisk in {{stone-ground-grits}} of grits in a slow stream. Reduce to the lowest heat and cook for 25–30 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes. Add hot water if they thicken too much. Finish with {{butter-unsalted}} of butter and {{cheddar}} of grated cheddar. Keep warm.

Render {{smoked-bacon}} of bacon cubes in a large frying pan over a medium heat until crisp. Remove and set aside. Cook the white parts of the spring onions and the green pepper in the bacon fat for 3 minutes. Add the garlic and paprika for 1 minute. Add the tinned tomatoes, {{chicken-stock}} of stock, Worcestershire sauce, and hot sauce. Simmer for 5 minutes.

Season {{king-prawn-raw}} of prawns with salt and pepper and add them to the sauce. Cook for 2–3 minutes, turning once, until pink and just done. Add lemon juice and the crisp bacon. Spoon grits into bowls, top with the shrimp and sauce, and scatter with spring onion greens and parsley.
